items.q: What is the behaviourist view on the nature-nurture debate?
items.correct: Strongly nurture - we are shaped by environmental conditioning.
items.why: Behaviourists reject innate ideas (tabula rasa - blank slate). Students soften this to 'a mix'. Watson and Skinner explicitly attributed nearly all behaviour to environment.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: How long does information typically last in short-term memory without rehearsal, according to Peterson and Peterson?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 18 seconds. “30 seconds” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose 1932 'War of the Ghosts' study demonstrated reconstructive memory?
items.why: Correct answer: Bartlett. “Atkinson” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Atkinson and Shiffrin propose the multi-store model?
items.why: The real figure is 1968 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
